title: Picard Ranks of Non-Ordinary K3 Surfaces
speaker: Ananth Shankar
abstract:
Let C be a curve defined over a finite field, and let X/C
 be a non-isotrivial family of K3 surfaces. In joint work with Maulik-Tang, under a compactness assumption (an assumption removed in later work by Tayou), we prove that if the K3 surface is generically ordinary, the locus where the Picard rank is greater than the generic Picard rank is infinite. There are examples of non-ordinary families of K3 surfaces where the locus where the Picard rank jumps is empty, and examples where this locus is finite. I will speak on joint work in progress with Ruofan Jiang and Ziquan Yang where we classify exactly when this locus is infinite under the hypothesis that the endomorphism ring of the generic Kuga-Satake abelian variety is determined by the generic Picard rank.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Known Picard-rank jumping in mixed and ordinary settings
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the elliptic-curve supersingularity analogy and reviews theorems saying that, for K3 surfaces over number fields and for generically ordinary K3 families in characteristic p, there are infinitely many reductions or specializations where the geometric Picard rank increases.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the naive non-ordinary extension
proof_stages.summary: The speaker gives counterexamples in supersingular, Kummer, and real-multiplication settings to show that non-ordinary K3 or abelian-surface families can have only finitely many Picard-rank jumps, so the desired theorem needs additional geometric exclusions.

proof_stages.label: Orthogonal Shimura varieties and special divisors
proof_stages.summary: The argument is recast in the language of GSpin/orthogonal Shimura varieties, Kuga–Satake maps, and special divisors/Noether–Lefschetz loci, with K3 moduli appearing as an instance of this orthogonal Shimura framework.

proof_stages.label: Likely-intersection principle and arithmetic avatars
proof_stages.summary: Picard-rank jumping is interpreted as an arithmetic likely-intersection problem for curves meeting infinitely many special divisors, supported by complex analogues, integral models, and applications such as ordinary Hecke-orbit density and K3 good-reduction criteria.

proof_stages.label: Newton-stratum obstruction detected by crystalline slopes
proof_stages.summary: Using the crystalline realization V and the lattice V_x of special endomorphism classes, the speaker explains that lying on a special divisor forces a slope-zero Frobenius class, while certain even-dimensional split Newton strata have no such slope and therefore meet special divisors only in the supersingular locus.

proof_stages.label: Non-ordinary intersection theorem with necessary hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The main non-ordinary results are stated: under avoidance of the bad Newton stratum and absence of extra generic Kuga–Satake endomorphisms, a curve in the Shimura variety meets the union of special divisors infinitely often, yielding Picard-rank jumps for appropriate non-ordinary K3 families.

examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that a Kummer surface attached to E × E has Picard-rank behavior governed by extra endomorphisms/CM, so rank jumps in reduction need not be detecting supersingularity.

examples.name: Supersingular non-isotrivial K3 family
examples.note: A family whose fibers are all supersingular has Picard rank 22 everywhere, showing that the naive non-ordinary analogue of the jumping theorem is false and forcing extra hypotheses.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses a supersingular non-isotrivial K3 family as a counterexample and states its Picard rank is constantly 22 without developing a construction or computation

passes.reason: A supersingular K3 family has maximal Picard rank 22 on every fiber and is presented as a counterexample to infinite rank jumping.
